UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile information within the Token Miner application. This entity stores personal details, but excludes authentication credentials.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

email.description: The user's email address, used for communication and as a unique identifier for login (via external auth system).

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the user profile was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the user profile was last updated.

UserWallet.title: UserWallet
UserWallet.description: Stores the user's mining progress and current balance of Param coins. Each user has one associated wallet.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserWallet entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile entity this wallet belongs to. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:1 UserWallet)

currentBalance.description: The total accumulated Param coins the user has mined. This value is always non-negative.

lastMinedAt.description: Timestamp of the last moment the user's mining progress was updated or actively recorded. Used to calculate accumulated coins for offline periods.

isMiningActive.description: A flag indicating if the user's mining process is currently considered active for real-time updates and coin accumulation.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the user wallet was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the user wallet was last updated.

definition.description: Stores a user's personal profile information. Each document's ID is the Firebase Authentication UID (`userId`). The 'id' field within the document is denormalized to match the document ID, enabling authorization independence and robust security rules for ownership verification without needing 'get()' operations.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of the user who owns this profile.

definition.description: Stores a user's mining progress and Param coin balance. Each document's ID is the Firebase Authentication UID (`userId`). The 'id' and 'userId' fields within the document are denormalized to match the document ID, enabling authorization independence and robust security rules for ownership verification without needing 'get()' operations.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique Firebase Authentication UID of the user who owns this wallet.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure is designed to be secure, scalable, and debuggable, adhering to the core design principles of Authorization Independence, Structural Segregation, Access Modeling, and Data Clarity.

**Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):**
Both `UserProfile` and `UserWallet` documents are stored under paths that directly incorporate the user's Firebase Authentication UID: `/user_profiles/{userId}` and `/user_wallets/{userId}` respectively. The document ID for each is explicitly set to the `request.auth.uid`. Furthermore, within each document, the `UserProfile`'s `id` field and the `UserWallet`'s `id` and `userId` fields are denormalized to match this `userId` (which is `request.auth.uid`). This critical denormalization strategy ensures that security rules for `read`, `write`, and `update` operations can verify ownership solely by comparing `request.auth.uid` with the `{userId}` in the path and the corresponding denormalized fields within `request.resource.data` (e.g., `request.auth.uid == userId && request.resource.data.userId == userId`). This completely eliminates the need for expensive and complex `get()` calls in security rules to check parent documents or other data, thus making rules atomic, faster, and easier to debug and reason about.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):**
Structural Segregation is applied by ensuring that each collection (e.g., `/user_profiles`, `/user_wallets`) holds documents with a homogeneous security posture: they are all private and accessible only by the owner. This means `list` operations on the root collections (`/user_profiles` or `/user_wallets`) will typically be denied by security rules. Users are expected to perform `get` operations on their specific, known document path (e.g., `/user_profiles/{{request.auth.uid}}`). By preventing `list` operations from returning unauthorized data, the structure implicitly supports QAPs, as clients cannot filter large sets of data, but must instead request specific, authorized documents. The responsibility for filtering lies with the client knowing the specific ID (their UID) they are authorized to access, rather than relying on the database to filter results after a broad query.
